title: Stop Saying Lice!

jokes.text: Okay, in my defense, I did not know he was a substitute teacher, and basically, nothing happened.
jokes.setup: Moira mentions she got an email from Alexis's school, implying an academic or conduct issue.
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately launches into a pre-emptive defense about a romantic/inappropriate incident with a substitute teacher — a completely different (and worse) problem than the one being raised.
jokes.explanation: A textbook misdirection: the audience and Moira expect school-related news, but Alexis's guilty conscience reveals something far more scandalous. The joke is deeply character-integrated — only Alexis would have this specific brand of preemptive confession. The economy is excellent; the payoff lands in one sentence. Earns its score because it's rooted in Alexis's established character, not a random non-sequitur.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I got an email from the school today.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, okay, before you say anything, it was totally consensual, and he said he'd never done anything like that before, so...

jokes.text: It's probably Kelsey, she's such a horse girl.
jokes.setup: The family reacts to news of a lice outbreak in Alexis's class.
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately identifies the culprit as 'a horse girl' — a very specific social archetype — with zero self-awareness about how judgmental she's being.
jokes.explanation: 'Horse girl' as a cultural archetype is a well-understood modern reference and lands as a recognizable observation. Alexis's confidence in her casual social cruelty is on-brand. Functional and funny without being exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I didn't call her horse-faced. I said she had a long face and smelled like a stable.

jokes.text: Dad, that's so mean! She doesn't look like a horse, she just talks about them a lot. And smells a bit.
jokes.setup: Johnny gently calls Alexis out for judging someone by their appearance.
jokes.punchline: Alexis defends Kelsey from the appearance criticism while simultaneously describing her in a way that's arguably worse — still smelling like a horse, just not looking like one.
jokes.explanation: The escalating structure is beautiful: Alexis genuinely thinks she's being kind, but each qualifier makes it worse. 'And smells a bit' is perfectly placed as the kicker. Strong character integration — Alexis's lack of self-awareness is the engine of the joke. Well-constructed two-beat setup.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God, did you just say lice? You all keep saying lice like it's... like it's a casual thing! Lice! LICE?! This is a nightmare!

jokes.text: You keep scratching. [Beat] No, I'm just allergic to the down pillow.
jokes.setup: The family is panicking about lice; Alexis notices Johnny scratching his head.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's instant defensive denial — 'I'm just allergic to the down pillow' — is transparently unconvincing, getting a laugh from the denial itself.
jokes.explanation: A solid sitcom beat — the denial is funnier than a straight admission would be, and the specificity of 'down pillow' adds a light character touch. Not especially original but well-executed in the moment.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, so Kelsey and I aren't actually friends. She was just doing charity work. Apparently, she photographs people with lice for her portfolio.

jokes.text: Wait a minute, we were in all the rooms changing the sheets!
jokes.setup: The family realizes Johnny probably has lice from Alexis.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's horror at his own situation is amplified by the realization that in trying to solve the problem, he may have spread it to every hotel room.
jokes.explanation: This escalation is clever because it extrapolates the lice problem to a much larger scale (a whole motel), turning personal embarrassment into a business crisis. Good structural escalation, and sets up the later sheet-changing scene.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I have a very specific preference for fresh linens. Twice daily. It's not unusual for someone of my... standards.

jokes.text: Can everyone stop saying lice please?!
jokes.setup: The family keeps loudly repeating the word 'lice' as the panic escalates.
jokes.punchline: David's plea — more horrified by the word itself than the actual problem — is a perfect expression of his particular brand of dramatic fastidiousness.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode title joke and it earns that distinction. The economy is near-perfect — David's entire personality is encapsulated in one sentence. His horror is more aesthetic than practical, and the line has become the most quotable moment of the episode. High character integration because only David would prioritize the linguistic experience over the actual parasitic threat.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Your hair looks... remarkably neat today.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Thank you, I blow-dried it myself.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, well, it's very... compact. Tightly wound, if you will. Almost impressively... hygienic.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Why do you keep saying that?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm just complimenting your... scalp management.

jokes.text: Okay, how do I know that she didn't give me her lice? [Alexis: You don't have lice.] Nobody here has lice! No, no, you do! You-you have the lice! Ah! Ughhh!
jokes.setup: David is convinced he has lice from Alexis; the family keeps denying it even as everyone starts panicking.
jokes.punchline: The chaos escalates to screaming and presumably physical recoiling as multiple family members shriek and scatter.
jokes.explanation: The collective meltdown is energetically funny but a bit chaotic on the page. The escalation works in performance but the individual beats aren't particularly crafted. The comedy here is more execution-dependent (actor physicality) than writing-dependent.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God, did you just... say hello to me? Without any trace of sarcasm or disdain? I'm genuinely moved. Should I sit down? I feel like I might need to sit down. This is the nicest thing that's ever happened to me.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm going to remember this moment forever. The day Stevie acknowledged my existence with basic human decency. I should write it down.

jokes.text: This is the last time I take a pity selfie with Kelsey!
jokes.setup: The chaos reaches a peak; Alexis realizes she likely got lice from Kelsey.
jokes.punchline: Her response isn't concern for Kelsey or herself — it's outrage at having lowered herself to take a 'pity selfie,' revealing her hierarchy of concerns.
jokes.explanation: 'Pity selfie' is a genuinely inspired coinage — it captures Alexis's entire social worldview in two words. The callback to Kelsey pays off the earlier horse girl setup. The implicit condescension baked into the phrase is Alexis in a nutshell.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I've really come into my own professionally. I mean, look at me now—I'm thriving. I'm like Oprah, except instead of a talk show and a production company, I have crippling anxiety and a store that sells used furniture.

jokes.text: Hello! Uh, just here for the complimentary... sheet change.
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to change sheets in a hotel room to prevent lice spread, but the guests are in the room.
jokes.punchline: Johnny improvises 'complimentary sheet change' as if it's a real motel service, an obvious lie that he commits to fully.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's hapless improvisation is a reliable sitcom engine — the flustered straight man digging himself deeper. 'Complimentary sheet change' is a believable-enough lie that reveals his desperation. The ellipsis before 'sheet change' is a nice written indicator of him stalling.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We could have a sleepover at your place. It would be very platonic.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's the saddest thing I've ever heard.

jokes.text: Oh, good, good, good! [Guest: Why is that good?] Well, it just means you're having a busy day, you know, up and at 'em.
jokes.setup: The guest says they haven't touched the sheets (meaning no lice transfer opportunity); Johnny blurts 'oh good, good, good' without thinking.
jokes.punchline: His improvised explanation — 'up and at 'em' — is so thin that it essentially admits he's hiding something, making the cringe worse.
jokes.explanation: The accidental revelation ('oh good!') followed by a scramble is a well-worn cringe structure that works here because of Johnny's specific character — a businessman trying desperately to maintain professional dignity while completely failing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm wearing a hat.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's a shower cap.

jokes.text: And your hair's in a ponytail, that's fantastic. By that I mean it's very hot outside, and gives the neck a chance to breathe.
jokes.setup: Johnny is desperately trying to fill dead air while looking for an excuse to stay in the room; he compliments the guest's ponytail.
jokes.punchline: He immediately realizes he's made it weird, so he offers an explanation that makes it even weirder — connecting a hairstyle to neck ventilation.
jokes.explanation: This is the highlight of the hotel room scene. The ponytail compliment is bizarre enough, but the instant overcorrection ('by that I mean') and the neck-breathing explanation turns it into something genuinely awkward and specific. Only a person deeply panicking would go here, and it's very Johnny.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't know what you're talking about.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: But if I did have lice, which I don't, it would be the fanciest lice in the town.

jokes.text: So that's a yes to changing the sheets? [Couple: No, no, we're good. Thanks.] After you. Oh, so leaving everything as is, then.
jokes.setup: The couple is clearly trying to leave and get Johnny out; Johnny can't accept that he's failed.
jokes.punchline: His final sad acknowledgment — 'so leaving everything as is, then' — is the defeated postmortem of a man who knows he's lost but needs to name it.
jokes.explanation: A solid button to the hotel room scene. Johnny's polite defeat is characteristic, though the laugh here is more of a comfortable chuckle than a big moment. Works as a coda.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: We got the old water tower, the gazebo in the town square, my bench down by the creek...

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Your bench? That's a teen hookup spot.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, it is now.

jokes.text: Hm! Are you talking to me? I'm not used to that ah, level of kindness.
jokes.setup: David arrives late to meet Patrick; Patrick says 'it's nice to see you' warmly.
jokes.punchline: David is so accustomed to adversarial interactions that genuine warmth registers as suspicious or foreign.
jokes.explanation: This is good character work — David's defensiveness toward genuine kindness reveals his emotional history while being played for laughs. The 'level of kindness' phrasing is nicely specific.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So I have this client, and they have lice. Like, really bad lice. They're very... um... they have brown hair. Or maybe blonde? And they're tall. Or average height. They live in a house. A big house. With windows. And they're very concerned about the lice situation.

jokes.text: I'm going to ignore that passive aggression, and instead, lean in to the fact that we're both working professionals now, and Mama Oprah would be very proud of us.
jokes.setup: Stevie notes they haven't seen each other in weeks — implying some distance has grown between them.
jokes.punchline: David reframes their professional separation as an Oprah-endorsed triumph, invoking 'Mama Oprah' as a spiritual authority figure.
jokes.explanation: 'Mama Oprah' is a genuinely inspired phrase — David using Oprah as a maternal spiritual touchstone is both absurd and completely character-consistent. The line has high quotability because it's so specific to David's worldview: celebrity-worshipping, defensive, but oddly sincere. 'Lean in' as corporate jargon deployed in a personal conversation is also a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, fine. There is no Meredith. I made her up.

jokes.text: Yeah um, that would be a shower cap.
jokes.setup: Stevie asks what's under the hat after the suspicious concealment.
jokes.punchline: David's almost-casual admission — 'that would be a shower cap' — delivered as if this is only slightly unusual, is the punchline to the visual gag.
jokes.explanation: The phrasing 'that would be a shower cap' is more interesting than a direct 'it's a shower cap' — it has a slight remove, as if David is identifying an object he's almost surprised to find on his own head. Maximum economy: three words of setup, five of punchline. Very David.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I'm not little. I've been going to the gym. I can bench press like 185 pounds.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's great, Ted.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, it would be more, but my wrists are very delicate.

jokes.text: So this would have nothing to do with... the lice outbreak at the motel? Your dad just called me.
jokes.setup: David has constructed an elaborate cover story about wanting to reconnect as friends.
jokes.punchline: Stevie deflates the entire charade in one sentence — she already knows, because Johnny called. The shower cap was all she needed.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's casual all-knowing takedown is a great beat — she's let him go on at length and had the answer all along. 'Your dad just called me' is the perfect kicker because it makes David's elaborate ruse doubly pointless.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I'm like a samurai with a lice comb.

jokes.text: I can't afford to have the store infested with lice.
jokes.setup: Stevie asks if David's desire to sleep over is truly not about the lice.
jokes.punchline: David breaks and admits the real reason — but frames it as a professional concern for the store, not personal squeamishness. His priorities are revealing.
jokes.explanation: Functional joke that reveals character priorities. David's concern for the store over his own health is funny but not especially crafted. Works as a character beat more than a standalone joke.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, I have to tell you something. I've been lying to you this whole time.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What do you mean you know? I just told you!

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I've known for a while. I was just waiting for you to tell me.

jokes.text: Bob: Who's gonna be the one to tell her? Roland: I'll handle this. Bob: Have I been let go? Should I leave? Roland: No. Bob: I won't make a scene.
jokes.setup: Moira enters the council meeting looking visually striking (presumably in an elaborate outfit); the men react before she notices.
jokes.punchline: Bob immediately assumes he's being fired — revealing his perpetual anxiety about his employment status — and pre-emptively promises not to make a scene while clearly already making one.
jokes.explanation: Bob's paranoid self-referential panic is funny and establishes his character quickly. 'I won't make a scene' while already making a scene is a nice ironic beat. Quick-hit ensemble comedy that works well.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Is this made from actual milk? Because I'm not putting dairy products all over my body.

jokes.text: You still have three and a half more years. [Bob: But who's counting?]
jokes.setup: Bob is worried he's been fired.
jokes.punchline: Roland confirms he still has 'three and a half more years' — a weirdly specific countdown that implies Bob's position has an end date they're all tracking, and Bob's 'but who's counting' reveals he very much is counting.
jokes.explanation: The 'three and a half more years' is oddly specific in a way that implies a whole backstory (a term? a contract? a curse?). Bob's rhetorical 'who's counting' when he clearly is makes for a tight, economical exchange.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's a scent. For men. It's called 'Herb Ertlinger's Fruit Liqueur.'

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: But that's a liqueur.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, the *fragrance* is called 'Herb Ertlinger's Fruit Liqueur.'

jokes.text: I mean, yeah, the whole town is named after me, and Bob over here, he's got his bench.
jokes.setup: Roland is making the case for how special it is to have something named after you in Schitt's Creek.
jokes.punchline: Roland positions himself (the entire town name) and Bob (a single bench) on the same sliding scale of tribute, with no apparent awareness of the disparity.
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy — Roland presents his own legacy (the town) and Bob's (a bench) with complete sincerity, treating them as comparable. The implicit absurdity of the Schitt's Creek name itself adds a meta layer. Roland's obliviousness is the engine.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, that's just common sense.

jokes.text: Bob's Bench. [Said reverently]
jokes.setup: Roland mentions that Bob has a bench named after him.
jokes.punchline: Bob says 'Bob's Bench' as if repeating the name of a great monument — with total sincerity about this extremely modest tribute.
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy: two words, all character. The reverential repetition of 'Bob's Bench' — as if it's the Louvre — captures Bob in his entirety. The joke is in the performance but the writing sets it up perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That was good.

jokes.text: And Ronnie's got her rec centre. [Ronnie: It's an outdoor tennis court, and last year the net was stolen.]
jokes.setup: Roland is listing tributes to local figures to make Moira feel the honor is special.
jokes.punchline: Ronnie's flat correction — it's just a tennis court with a stolen net — demolishes Roland's buildup with one sentence of brutal bathos.
jokes.explanation: Ronnie's deadpan correction is very funny — she has no illusions about her 'rec centre' and delivers the correction without self-pity. 'And last year the net was stolen' adds a perfect kicker of ongoing indignity. Excellent bathos.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You should check your hair regularly for lice. It's just a precaution.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Oh my God, I have lice. I'm infested. I'm going to have to shave my head.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You don't have lice, David. I'm just saying you should check.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You literally just told me I should check for lice, which means you think I have lice. Why would you tell me to check if you didn't think I had them?

jokes.text: Well, she's kind of under selling it there, the rec centre is a major hook-up spot for the kids.
jokes.setup: Ronnie has described her 'rec centre' as a dilapidated tennis court with a stolen net.
jokes.punchline: Roland's defense of the rec centre — that it's a teenage hookup spot — is not the endorsement he thinks it is.
jokes.explanation: Roland's oblivious endorsement — 'it's a hookup spot!' as a compliment — is solid character comedy. Works well as a continuation of the bathos from Ronnie's line.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm gonna go get some wine and just... watch this happen.

jokes.text: So is the bench.
jokes.setup: Roland has just said the rec centre is a teen hookup spot.
jokes.punchline: Ronnie's flat 'so is the bench' recontextualizes Bob's Bench as another teen hookup location — adding a layer of indignity to Bob's monument.
jokes.explanation: Three words. Perfect economy, perfect callback, perfect deadpan. 'So is the bench' recontextualizes Bob's proud monument as a teen makeout spot, delivered with zero inflection by Ronnie. This is the kind of microjoke that epitomizes Schitt's Creek's comedic efficiency. Justifies its 8+ score: it's the perfect callback punchline delivered with maximum restraint.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A theatre named after some random volunteer usher? Who the fuck is that?

jokes.text: As much as I would love to contribute to the population growth of this town by way of teen pregnancies, I must decline.
jokes.setup: Roland and Bob have been trying to sell Moira on the garden by listing other local tributes, including teen hookup spots.
jokes.punchline: Moira's response acknowledges the teen pregnancy subtext and declines in the most formal, theatrical register possible.
jokes.explanation: Moira's formal theatrical diction applied to something as base as teen pregnancy is a perfect expression of her character. 'Contribute to the population growth of this town by way of teen pregnancies' is both absurd and a precise logical extension of the preceding conversation. 'I must decline' as a close is perfectly portentous. This is Moira at her comedic peak: finding the most grandiose possible framing for a simple 'no.'

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Pacifying pablum? A garden is not pacifying pablum!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A garden is a gift of grace and generosity, not some perfunctory, patronizing, pitiful pacification!

jokes.text: Honey, it's all about legacy! Your name will forever be associated with this town's name!
jokes.setup: Roland is trying to convince Moira that having a garden named after her is a great honor.
jokes.punchline: The unintentional joke: 'Moira's garden' being associated with 'Schitt's Creek' is not the flattering legacy Roland thinks it is. The town name itself undermines his entire pitch.
jokes.explanation: The meta-joke about the town name doing the opposite of what Roland intends is built into the show's DNA. It works, though it requires the audience to make the connection rather than the writing doing the heavy lifting.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A garden? Absolutely not. I'm not about to spend my time cultivating vegetables when I can barely cultivate a relationship with my daughter. Besides, the last time I saw something that overgrown and unkempt, it was Alexis's head after she came back from that wellness retreat. I'm pretty sure I saw a family of lice setting up a summer home in there.

jokes.text: Does she want me to help her? [Alexis: No.] [Long beat]
jokes.setup: Alexis has made up a fictional client 'Meredith' with lice, implying she'd be too embarrassed to ask Ted for help.
jokes.punchline: Ted's simple question 'does she want me to help her?' — and Alexis's flat 'no' — is a beat where the audience sees through the fiction before Ted does. His confused pause lands the joke.
jokes.explanation: The awkward pause after 'no' — where Ted presumably processes that no one wants his help while Alexis scrambles — is good beat comedy. The joke is in the gap between what's said and what's meant.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I thought you said don't change the sheets, I heard 'go ahead and change the sheets.' It's a very common mix-up.

jokes.text: And I was like... Mere-dith, Meredith, there's no reason to be embarrassed, you're still exceptionally attractive, it's just lice, just get the shampoo, and deal with it!
jokes.setup: Alexis is narrating what she told the fictional 'Meredith' about her lice.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's pep talk to herself — 'you're still exceptionally attractive, it's just lice' — reveals that her primary concern is her own attractiveness, not the medical issue.
jokes.explanation: Alexis giving herself a pep talk through a fictional third party is a lovely character moment. 'You're still exceptionally attractive' as the lead comfort — before any practical advice — perfectly captures her hierarchy of values. The Meredith repetition has a nice desperate quality.

dialogue.speaker: Guest
dialogue.line: How old is your daughter?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: She's 28.

jokes.text: I'm kind of a samurai when it comes to a metal comb, so...
jokes.setup: Ted is explaining how to deal with lice — specifically combing them out.
jokes.punchline: Ted characterizing himself as a 'samurai' with a lice comb is both endearing and slightly absurd — an unexpected image from a mild-mannered vet.
jokes.explanation: 'Samurai when it comes to a metal comb' is a funny image and the 'so...' trailing off implies he's genuinely offering to help with a sort of modest pride. Ted's gentle confidence is on-brand.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I may have gone through some of the guests' personal items.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: But only the ones that were left out.

jokes.text: Um, you know that it's me, right? [Ted: Yeah, caught on pretty quick.]
jokes.setup: Alexis has been talking about the fictional 'Meredith' throughout the scene; she finally breaks.
jokes.punchline: Ted's 'caught on pretty quick' reveals he saw through the entire charade immediately but chose to play along — showing his patience and fondness for Alexis.
jokes.explanation: A warm and funny payoff to the Meredith bit. Ted's 'caught on pretty quick' has a gentle irony — 'pretty quick' meaning immediately. The line rewards the audience who was waiting for the reveal, and the warmth of Ted's patience makes it land even better.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I just have this image of myself as a well-toned bug woman, you know? Like, really fit. Just crawling around.

jokes.text: You're the sweetest little vet I've ever met. [Ted: I don't know about little, because I'm benching 225 right now, so it's not really something someone little...]
jokes.setup: Alexis calls Ted 'the sweetest little vet' as a term of affection.
jokes.punchline: Ted immediately corrects the 'little' with a very specific gym stat — 'benching 225' — revealing that despite his gentle demeanor, he's sensitive about this particular descriptor.
jokes.explanation: 'Benching 225' is a very specific and funny detail — Ted's gentle masculinity subtext has been present throughout the show and this is a great expression of it. The precision of '225' (not 'a lot,' but an exact amount) makes it funnier. Alexis's 'Aaah, Ted!' is the perfect button.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I just wanted you to think I was cool, okay? I didn't want you to know that I was just some well-toned bug woman living in a motel room.

jokes.text: Can you drink these? [Patrick: Um, it's liquid moisturizer, for your body, so no, you can't drink them.]
jokes.setup: Roland is sorting bottles labeled 'body milk' in the store.
jokes.punchline: Roland reads 'body milk' and reasonably (to him) wonders if it's drinkable. His question is both absurd and weirdly logical.
jokes.explanation: Roland's sincere practical question about body milk sets up the David meta-joke nicely. The question itself is funny from Roland's perspective — he approaches all products empirically.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: It could be worse. I could shear you like an animal.

jokes.text: Did you ask if you can drink it, too? [David: Okay, it says body milk on the label!]
jokes.setup: Roland has just asked if body milk is drinkable; Patrick has explained it's moisturizer.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's question implies David asked the same thing — retroactively making David and Roland equivalently confused, which David finds humiliating.
jokes.explanation: The reveal that David — the store owner — made the same mistake as Roland is a perfect comedic indignity for David. His defensive 'it says body milk on the label!' both confirms the answer is yes and demonstrates his own bad label decision. Great callback structure.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, this is reminding me of this restaurant I went to in New York. It was like, a farm-to-table situation, but instead of a farm, it was just a guy's scalp, and instead of a table, it was... also a scalp.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: That doesn't sound like a real restaurant.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, it was very real. Very exclusive. You had to have lice to get in.

jokes.text: You know, I told David that the label was gonna be misleading, but he insisted. What was it you said? 'Anyone with a fiber of common sense would know that it's not actually milk.'
jokes.setup: David is defending the 'body milk' label after both Roland and apparently himself were confused by it.
jokes.punchline: Stevie quotes David's own dismissive words back at him, perfectly timed to destroy his credibility.
jokes.explanation: Stevie turning David's own smugness against him is a beautifully structured comedic moment. 'Anyone with a fiber of common sense' is a great quotable phrase that gets even funnier in context. The dramatic irony — the audience knows David asked the same question Roland did — makes the payoff devastating.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So anyway, the health inspector comes in, and there's just lice everywhere. In the food, in the kitchen...

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: You know, it's interesting you mention that, because I'm finding lice everywhere too.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ted!

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, your restaurant's hygiene problem and your current situation aren't that different.

jokes.text: What do we think body milk is, if not milk for your body?!
jokes.setup: David has just been destroyed by Stevie quoting his own words back at him.
jokes.punchline: David's tortured logic — 'milk for your body' makes complete sense to him — both defends his position and reveals the flaw in the label simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: David's outraged rhetorical question is the perfect button to the body milk sequence. 'What do we think body milk is, if not milk for your body?!' captures both his defensiveness and his blind spot — he's simultaneously right and wrong, and the exasperation makes it funnier. High quotability because it's a satisfying non-sequitur that also makes complete sense.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Oh my God, look at the size of this louse. This must be the mama.

jokes.text: Stevie, right? I'm Patrick. I've heard a lot about you. [Stevie: None of it is true.] Oh well, anyone with a fiber of common sense would know that.
jokes.setup: Patrick introduces himself to Stevie for the first time.
jokes.punchline: Patrick immediately weaponizes Stevie's own catchphrase (which she's never actually said) by quoting David's 'fiber of common sense' line back in a context that confirms he's already gotten a thorough briefing on Stevie.
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the body milk sequence and one of the episode's standout moments. Patrick uses the exact phrase Stevie just deployed — 'anyone with a fiber of common sense' — to both deflect her deflection AND demonstrate that he knows her. It's a triple callback (David said it, Stevie quoted it, Patrick quotes the quote) deployed in a first meeting. The structure is exceptional: it works on multiple levels simultaneously. Stevie's delighted 'I like him' is the earned punctuation. Justifies 8.6: the architecture of this joke is genuinely impressive.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have to say, I'm pleasantly surprised by your apartment. It's not at all what I imagined.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What did you imagine?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, something a bit more... spartan. Maybe some exposed wiring, a single lightbulb, possibly a bucket in the corner for... various purposes.

jokes.text: I like him. I like you.
jokes.setup: Patrick has just perfectly deployed Stevie's own language against her on a first meeting.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's rapid first-person and second-person switch — addressing David, then directly Patrick — is a funny expression of immediate approval, especially since Stevie is stingy with approval.
jokes.explanation: Two sentences, maximum impact. Stevie's instant pivot from commentary to direct address is charming and efficient. It's a reaction beat that also establishes Stevie and Patrick's dynamic from the first moment.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'll have you know I've been to every Sarah McLachlan Lilith Fair since 1998. It was a formative experience for me as a young man exploring my identity and celebrating female artists.

jokes.text: Okay, is this how this is gonna go? Because we have way too much work to do today for me to feel attacked by way of an imbalanced social dynamic.
jokes.setup: Stevie and Patrick have immediately bonded, leaving David slightly sidelined.
jokes.punchline: David's wounded response frames a moment of mild social discomfort as an 'imbalanced social dynamic' — an overblown descriptor for being slightly left out.
jokes.explanation: 'Attacked by way of an imbalanced social dynamic' is a wonderfully overwrought way to describe being left out, and very David. The therapy-speak framing of a minor interpersonal slight is signature character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Oh my God, oh my God, oh my God.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Is that a men's hair product? In Stevie's bathroom?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David, what are you doing in here?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I was looking for a Q-tip and I found... evidence of a romantic entanglement.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's Patrick's.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: Are-are you wearing a shower cap?!
jokes.setup: The store conversation has progressed; Patrick finally notices what's on David's head.
jokes.punchline: The delayed recognition — Patrick has presumably been talking to David for a while before noticing — makes the callback to the shower cap land harder.
jokes.explanation: Good callback — the shower cap has been mentioned but now Patrick actually sees it for the first time. The stutter ('are-are you') suggests genuine confusion before comprehension. Functional and funny.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What is this? A sad collection of... is this chicken? Or was it chicken? Why would anyone do this to themselves?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's meal prep.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Meal prep? This looks more like a cry for help in Tupperware form.

jokes.text: Oh, he doesn't have it, I checked his head.
jokes.setup: Patrick has discovered David is wearing a shower cap due to the lice situation.
jokes.punchline: Stevie casually mentioning she's already checked David's head — as if this is a normal thing friends do — is a funny revelation of intimacy.
jokes.explanation: The casualness of 'I checked his head' as a thing between friends is funny. Stevie's matter-of-fact delivery of something slightly intimate is very on-brand for her character.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Oh my God, oh my God, oh my God.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Stevie, is this... is this vodka?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's just vodka.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Just vodka? JUST vodka? Do you understand what you have here? This is a miracle. This is a genuine miracle.

jokes.text: I think the shower cap is more of a fashion choice at this point.
jokes.setup: David is wearing a shower cap despite being lice-free.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's dry observation — that it's transitioned from medical precaution to fashion — gently roasts David's eccentric aesthetic sensibility.
jokes.explanation: The joke that David would wear a shower cap as a fashion choice — and that Stevie would say this with a straight face — is a lovely small character moment. Works well.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I've been honored with a street name, a park bench, and most recently, a road.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A road?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well, it was more of an evacuation route, but I renamed it.

jokes.text: It's almost as if you want me to get the lice.
jokes.setup: Patrick is pointing out that David could still get lice because he lives with Alexis.
jokes.punchline: David's paranoid interpretation of Patrick's practical concern — that Patrick is somehow rooting for his infestation — is a funny neurotic leap.
jokes.explanation: David's suspicious read of Patrick's concern is an early, funny window into David's tendency to find threat in kindness — a character trait that will have resonance later.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh no, not again. It's that time of year.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Jocelyn, honey, you need to stop eating those macaroons.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I know, but they're so good! Last year it was just a little rash.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: A little rash? You looked like a tomato.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, this year is different. I can't feel my tongue.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: We're calling an ambulance!

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: No, no, I'm fine. I'm... I'm not fine. Is the room spinning?

jokes.text: You can crash at my place tonight if you need to. [David: Thank you. But Stevie offered her place this morning.] Oh. [Pause] Can I crash at your place?
jokes.setup: Patrick offers his place to David as a lice-safe refuge; David reveals Stevie already offered.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's immediate pivot to asking if HE can crash at Stevie's is a funny status reversal — he went from magnanimous host to also wanting refuge in one beat.
jokes.explanation: Patrick's quick shift from benefactor to moocher is a sharp comedic beat that also establishes him as more relaxed and self-interested than his professional polish suggests. Well-structured.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh wow, a guest bedroom. What are you, a billionaire?

jokes.text: This is really fun for me. I'm having a lot of fun.
jokes.setup: Both David and Patrick are now vying for/discussing Stevie's hospitality.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's flat self-commentary — delivered without visible joy — is the deadpan response to finding herself suddenly popular.
jokes.explanation: A classic Stevie deadpan moment. The repetition ('really fun... a lot of fun') while presumably expressionless is very funny. Economy is excellent — she essentially refuses to elaborate on a feeling she may or may not have.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Is this... is this supposed to say something? Because what I'm reading is 'It's the's garden's of the's Roses.' That's not... that's not how apostrophes work. At all.

jokes.text: Oh, I've got that playhouse theatre in Pasadena, my titular scholarship with Phoenix, David and Alexis.
jokes.setup: Johnny suggests Moira should think twice about turning down the garden tribute.
jokes.punchline: Moira lists her legacies and includes 'David and Alexis' as achievements on par with a theatre and a scholarship — treating her children as accomplishments rather than people.
jokes.explanation: Including her children in a list of legacy achievements is a perfect Moira moment — it's simultaneously loving and narcissistic. The ordering matters: theatre, scholarship, then David and Alexis, implying they're the least impressive of the three. Strongly character-integrated.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What is this sign supposed to say?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I think it's a garden belonging to multiple people named Alexis?

jokes.text: About a month ago I heard from the Lucy Albion Studio Theatre in Pasadena. [Moira: I don't know what that is.] It used to be called the Moira Rose... [Moira: No, no! Don't say it!]
jokes.setup: Johnny has been building up to revealing something about Pasadena; Moira says she doesn't know the Lucy Albion Studio Theatre.
jokes.punchline: Moira's instant 'don't say it' — before Johnny can finish — reveals she already knows what he's about to say, and her preemptive horror is funnier than the reveal itself would be.
jokes.explanation: The structure here is excellent: we're set up with the legacy speech, then the name-change is revealed through a conversation where Moira insists she doesn't know the theatre — and then instantly recognizes it. Her preemptive 'don't say it' is the best reaction she could have. The interrupted revelation is better than the reveal itself. Justifies the 8.0.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so this whole time your birthday is just the numbers from your credit card?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I couldn't remember it, and my card was right there.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Dad, that's... that's not how birthdays work.

jokes.text: They've stripped the playhouse of my name? Who the fuck is Lucy Albion?!
jokes.setup: Moira has just realized her name has been removed from the Pasadena theatre.
jokes.punchline: 'Who the fuck is Lucy Albion?!' — Moira's outraged question is both funny in its profanity (rare for her theatrical register) and in being immediately answered.
jokes.explanation: 'Who the fuck is Lucy Albion?!' is Moira at her most entertainingly petty, and the profanity punctuates the theatrical outrage beautifully. The setup-punchline where her rhetorical 'who is she' is immediately and deflatingy answered makes it a classic double beat.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Are we going to be buried here?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I don't know, David. I hadn't really thought about it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I don't want to be. I think it's morbid.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, David, don't be so dramatic.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not being dramatic. I just don't want to spend eternity in Schitt's Creek.

jokes.text: A volunteer usher who died last year.
jokes.setup: Moira demands to know who Lucy Albion is.
jokes.punchline: The flat answer — a dead volunteer usher — is the most deflating possible response to Moira's grandiose outrage. She's been replaced by someone she certainly considers beneath her, and who can't even defend themselves.
jokes.explanation: Seven words. Maximum devastation. 'A volunteer usher who died last year' is a perfect punchline: it's dark (she can't be mad at a dead person), it's deflating (Moira replaced by a volunteer!), and it's delivered by Johnny in the most matter-of-fact way possible. The economy here is near-perfect. Justifies its 8.2 score: it's one of the best-constructed single-line punchlines in the episode.

jokes.text: And when were you planning on telling me this, John?! [Johnny: Well Moira, it's not like you have overreacted to bad news in the past. I just thought it might sit better with you once you'd heard about the garden.]
jokes.setup: Moira is outraged that Johnny kept the theatre name-change from her.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's explanation — 'it's not like you have overreacted to bad news in the past' (meaning she absolutely has) and his garden-as-buffer strategy — reveals he's been running a psychological softening operation.
jokes.explanation: The revelation that Johnny engineered the entire garden tribute as emotional cushioning for the theatre news is a great structural payoff. His sardonic 'it's not like you have overreacted to bad news' is delivered with perfect dry awareness. The scene recontextualizes everything before it.

jokes.text: No, I just told you about the garden. [Johnny: Uh-huh.] John, did you ask Roland and Bob to name a garden after me?
jokes.setup: Moira is catching up to the fact that the garden was Johnny's doing.
jokes.punchline: Moira's dawning realization — and Johnny's guilty 'uh-huh' — is funny because she's just now connecting dots Johnny thought were obvious.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's patient 'uh-huh' while Moira connects the dots is a classic long-suffering husband beat. Functional comedically.

jokes.text: Oh, I won't be pitied, John! Or fed your pacifying pablum like some kind of... soft-headed infant!
jokes.setup: Johnny reveals the garden was meant to soften the blow of losing the theatre.
jokes.punchline: 'Pacifying pablum like some kind of soft-headed infant' — Moira's theatrical rage produces an accidentally self-undermining metaphor as she describes herself.
jokes.explanation: 'Pacifying pablum like some kind of soft-headed infant' is an exceptional piece of Moira dialogue — alliterative, theatrical, and self-referentially accurate. Pablum (baby food) + soft-headed infant creates an image that is simultaneously grandiose and ridiculous. Only Moira's specific vocabulary could produce this phrase. The alliteration of 'pacifying pablum' is a genuine writing flourish. Justifies 8.0.

jokes.text: Well, Alexis has lice. [Moira: What?! Ewwww! I assume you shaved her head! And we have to put a wig on her. But not one of my wigs! These have to be boxed now!]
jokes.setup: After a heated argument about the garden deception, Johnny tries to end the conversation.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's casual postscript — 'well, Alexis has lice' — pivots the scene entirely, and Moira's cascade of increasingly specific demands (shave head, wig, not MY wigs, box them NOW) is pure escalating character comedy.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's casual late-mention of the lice is funny as a tonal shift. But Moira's escalating response — shave her, wig her, not MY wigs, box them NOW — is the real comedy, each beat getting more specifically Moira. The wigs being treated as more precious than her daughter is a dark but perfectly in-character comic beat.

jokes.text: And I was worried about you overreacting.
jokes.setup: Moira has just delivered a cascade of hysterical demands about the lice.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's dry button — sarcastically referencing his earlier concern about her overreacting — lands as the perfect comic close to the scene.
jokes.explanation: Perfect scene button. The callback lands because it was set up earlier and the payoff is immediately earned by Moira's reaction. Johnny's delivery — presumably flat — is everything. Economy is excellent: one sentence closes the scene with perfect irony.

jokes.text: Oh, you said not to change the sheets! I could've sworn I heard you say change the sheets.
jokes.setup: Johnny has been caught in another hotel room trying to change the sheets; the guests tell him they said NOT to change them.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's 'I could've sworn I heard you say change the sheets' — a transparent lie — is a return to the hotel cringe comedy.
jokes.explanation: Functional callback to the earlier hotel scene. Not as inventive as the first encounter but works as a running gag. The lie is less elaborate this time, making it slightly weaker.

jokes.text: My daughter and I were setting up the room earlier, and it turns out she has lice. I was changing the sheets because you know, I didn't wanna take a chance. [Guest: Been there.] Yeah, our six year old daughter had lice last year. How old's yours? [Johnny: 28.]
jokes.setup: Johnny finally comes clean to the hotel guests about why he's been trying to change their sheets; the guest is sympathetic and shares their experience.
jokes.punchline: The guest asks 'how old's yours?' expecting a small child — Johnny's '28' is the deflating answer that reveals the absurdity of a 28-year-old giving her father a lice problem.
jokes.explanation: One of the sharper jokes in the episode. The setup is perfect: the guest creates a frame (sharing parenting lice woes) that implies childhood, and '28' detonates that assumption completely. The joke works on multiple levels: Alexis's immaturity, Johnny's long-suffering parenthood, and the absurdity of adult lice. Well-structured and earns its score.

jokes.text: Ted, a bug literally fell out of my head, onto my textbook this morning. It was so disgusting. Plus I didn't want you to picture me like some well-toned bug woman.
jokes.setup: Ted asks why Alexis didn't just tell him she had lice instead of making up 'Meredith.'
jokes.punchline: 'Well-toned bug woman' — Alexis's concern is not about embarrassment in general but specifically about the image of a physically fit person with lice. Her vanity is the engine.
jokes.explanation: 'Well-toned bug woman' is a genuinely great phrase — Alexis's specific anxiety is not about lice per se, but about the image combination of physical attractiveness and parasites. It's such a specific and revealing form of vanity. The 'literally fell out of my head' setup adds an extra layer of gross comedy before the punchline. High character integration: this phrase could only come from Alexis. Justifies 8.1.

jokes.text: Well, I've seen worse. Fleas, ticks, worms. Granted, all of those were on house pets.
jokes.setup: Alexis is embarrassed about the lice; Ted tries to reassure her.
jokes.punchline: Ted's reassurance list — 'fleas, ticks, worms' — followed by 'all on house pets' is a failed comfort that inadvertently compares Alexis to animals.
jokes.explanation: The structure is tight: a list of reassuring examples, then the qualifier that torpedoes the reassurance. Ted's vet brain can't stop itself from applying animal medicine context to Alexis. Very character-specific and well-timed.

jokes.text: I could be shearing you with trimmers right now.
jokes.setup: Ted is trying to cheer Alexis up about the lice treatment by pointing out it could be worse.
jokes.punchline: Ted's 'it could be worse — I could be shearing you' again applies animal husbandry to Alexis, treating her as a patient rather than a person.
jokes.explanation: Ted's animal-vet-applied-to-Alexis comedy is a running beat in this scene and 'shearing with trimmers' is the escalated version. Works well in the sequence.

jokes.text: It reminds me of when I was in Bangkok. There was this amazing little Resto-Lounge that specialized in Tahitian food, and scalp massages.
jokes.setup: Ted is combing out Alexis's lice — an intimate but medically unglamorous procedure.
jokes.punchline: Alexis compares the experience to a luxury Bangkok spa, obliviously aestheticizing a parasite removal. The 'Resto-Lounge specializing in Tahitian food and scalp massages' is absurd and very Alexis.
jokes.explanation: 'Resto-Lounge specializing in Tahitian food and scalp massages' is a wonderfully specific absurdist detail from Alexis's past life. The combination is bizarre enough to be funny on its own, and Alexis's total lack of irony about it makes it better. Ted's 'I'm not sure what's more disgusting, what I'm doing right now, or the concept for that restaurant' is an excellent deadpan response.

jokes.text: I'm not sure what's more disgusting, what I'm doing right now, or the concept for that restaurant.
jokes.setup: Alexis has compared the lice combing to a Bangkok Resto-Lounge scalp massage.
jokes.punchline: Ted's flat equivalence — placing lice removal and a Tahitian food/scalp massage combo on the same disgust scale — is a lovely deadpan.
jokes.explanation: Ted's flat comparative disgust is perfectly calibrated. 'I'm not sure what's more disgusting' as a sentence structure commits to genuine uncertainty between lice removal and a bizarre restaurant concept, which is the joke.

jokes.text: It was a Resto-Lounge.
jokes.setup: Ted has called the Bangkok establishment a 'restaurant.'
jokes.punchline: Alexis's correction — 'it was a Resto-Lounge' — delivered as if the distinction is important, is a perfect one-line character joke.
jokes.explanation: Four words, maximum character specificity. Alexis correcting 'restaurant' with 'Resto-Lounge' as if it's an important distinction captures everything about her: the entitled insistence on precision about luxury experiences she's had, the absurdity of the term itself, and the obliviousness to why anyone would find this funny. Maximum economy — this is a 10 on economy. Justifies 8.2.

jokes.text: Speaking of Thailand, I think this is probably the most intimate thing I've ever done with an employee.
jokes.setup: Ted has been combing lice from Alexis's hair — an objectively intimate act — and pivots to a Thailand non-sequitur.
jokes.punchline: 'Speaking of Thailand' connects to nothing — it's a false transition that highlights the awkwardness of the moment, and 'most intimate thing with an employee' underscores the weird professional-personal blur.
jokes.explanation: 'Speaking of Thailand' as a non-sequitur transition is funny in itself. The observation about intimacy-with-employee sets up Alexis's correction nicely.

jokes.text: Speaking of Thailand, I think we've done a few more intimate things than this. [Ted: I meant while they were an employee. Not that I'd do this for anyone else. Because it's actually pretty gross.]
jokes.setup: Ted says lice-combing is the most intimate thing he's done with an employee.
jokes.punchline: Alexis (a former employee) reminds him of their romantic history; Ted walks it back by saying 'while they were an employee' and then immediately undermines his tenderness by calling what he's doing 'pretty gross.'
jokes.explanation: A nice multi-beat exchange: Ted's caveat ('while they were an employee') is sweet but the kicker ('because it's actually pretty gross') undercuts the sweetness immediately. Well-structured exchange that reveals both characters.

jokes.text: Well, there are some big ones in here. [Alexis: Ewww!] I'm joking, I'm joking. Oh, maybe not. [Alexis: Eww!] Nope, it's fine. It is fine, it's fine. Just the mama, we got her.
jokes.setup: Ted has said the lice treatment isn't that bad; Alexis challenges that.
jokes.punchline: Ted's false alarm sequence — 'I'm joking... oh, maybe not... nope, it's fine... just the mama' — is a perfectly escalating cringe beat that ends in the horrifying term 'the mama.'
jokes.explanation: The escalating false reassurance — 'I'm joking / maybe not / nope fine / THE MAMA' — is a well-constructed cringe sequence. 'We got her' treating a louse like a fugitive is the dark comic kicker. Good structure.

jokes.text: This is a lot nicer than I expected. For some reason I pictured you living underground. In like, a bomb shelter? Yeah like, bomb shelter aspects.
jokes.setup: David arrives at Stevie's apartment for the first time.
jokes.punchline: David's preconception of Stevie as someone who would live in a bomb shelter — and then specifically qualifying it with 'aspects' — is a funny window into how he sees her.
jokes.explanation: 'Bomb shelter aspects' is a wonderfully specific hedge — David is backpedaling on an insult while still landing it. The image of Stevie in an underground bomb shelter is funny and reveals something about their dynamic. Good character specificity.

jokes.text: Look at what you've done to this place! Look at all this stuff! Look at that frame on the wall that you put there. Look at that Sarah McLachlan poster!
jokes.setup: David is faux-enthusiastically taking in Stevie's apartment.
jokes.punchline: His observations — a frame on the wall, a poster — are basic apartment features he's describing as if they're achievements. 'A frame on the wall that you put there' is both passive-aggressive and funny in its specificity.
jokes.explanation: David's backhanded enthusiasm for bare-minimum apartment decor is a rich character beat. 'A frame on the wall that you put there' as a noted achievement is particularly sharp.

jokes.text: Who's dissing Sarah McLachlan? I followed Lilith Fair for two summers. So...
jokes.setup: Stevie tells David not to dis Sarah McLachlan.
jokes.punchline: David's instant defense of Sarah McLachlan — I followed Lilith Fair for two summers — is an unexpected reveal of genuine musical allegiance beneath his ironic detachment.
jokes.explanation: 'I followed Lilith Fair for two summers' is a great character revelation — delivered with total sincerity. The 'So...' trailing off implies this ends any discussion. It's a subversion of David's assumed ironic positioning and reveals his authentic enthusiasms. Well-written.

jokes.text: Uh-oh! Two toothbrushes? Hello!
jokes.setup: David is exploring Stevie's apartment and spots something interesting in the bathroom.
jokes.punchline: The 'uh-oh' and 'hello!' framing of two toothbrushes signals David has immediately detected a romantic situation — his nosiness expressed through comedy.
jokes.explanation: Classic snoop-detects-secret comedy. The exclamations are fun and in character. Sets up the toothbrush subplot. Functional.

jokes.text: So maybe I was seeing somebody a while ago, and forgot to throw out his toothbrush.
jokes.setup: David has found two toothbrushes; Stevie needs to explain.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's casual admission — 'maybe I was seeing somebody' — and the non-explanation of not throwing out the brush is revealing of her character's emotional avoidance.
jokes.explanation: The 'forgot to throw out his toothbrush' as emotionally avoidant behavior is a good character beat for Stevie. Comedy is mild but the character revelation is genuine.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, is that a soft, yet affirmative indication that you miss me?
jokes.setup: Stevie implies David's been too busy with the store to spend time with her.
jokes.punchline: David translates Stevie's deflective comment into therapy-speak: 'soft, yet affirmative indication that you miss me' — both precise and fussy.
jokes.explanation: 'Soft, yet affirmative indication' is very David — overly precise clinical language applied to emotional subtext. Captures his tendency to both analyze and deflect genuine connection.

jokes.text: Okay, I just see two de-shelled hard-boiled eggs in a bag, should I be scared for you?
jokes.setup: David is looking in Stevie's fridge for drinks.
jokes.punchline: The specific detail — 'de-shelled hard-boiled eggs in a bag' — is a vivid and depressing image of bachelor fridge minimalism, and David's concern is genuine.
jokes.explanation: 'De-shelled hard-boiled eggs in a bag' is a precise and funny image — the 'de-shelled' specification makes it more clinical and therefore funnier. The phrase 'should I be scared for you' is David's combination of concern and judgment. Specific, visual, and earned.

jokes.text: Oh, in the freezer, okay. There she is! Look at that, look at her, go. What a gift.
jokes.setup: David finds the vodka in the freezer after the depressing fridge contents.
jokes.punchline: David's greeting of the vodka bottle — 'There she is! Look at her, go. What a gift.' — as if welcoming a person, is a perfect expression of his priorities.
jokes.explanation: David anthropomorphizing the vodka bottle — 'there she is,' 'what a gift' — contrasted with his horror at the eggs, is a lovely character beat. The reverence for the bottle is funny and specific.

jokes.text: Yes, it can be very exciting when the tribute matches the contribution. My name graced a theatre, a country club ladies' locker room, a roadway.
jokes.setup: A townsperson mentions that she was thrilled to have her name on a school play centre.
jokes.punchline: Moira's catalog of her own tributes — theatre, country club ladies' locker room, a roadway — is both grandiose and slightly absurd (a locker room?), delivered as if these are obviously more significant.
jokes.explanation: The list of tributes has a beautiful odd member — 'country club ladies' locker room' — that slightly undercuts the grandeur of theatre and roadway. Moira includes it with total sincerity, which is the joke.

jokes.text: Technically it was referred to as Evacuation Route 14, but those of us in the know affectionately called it... 'Moira Rose Boulevard.'
jokes.setup: Moira claimed to have a road named after her.
jokes.punchline: The 'road' was actually Evacuation Route 14 — a functional emergency designation — but Moira insists 'those of us in the know' called it Moira Rose Boulevard, implying she possibly named it herself.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's finest moments. 'Evacuation Route 14' — a purely functional road designation — becoming 'Moira Rose Boulevard' in Moira's retelling is perfect character comedy. 'Those of us in the know' implies an audience that possibly consisted only of Moira herself. The gap between the reality (emergency exit route) and her narrative (a prestigious boulevard) is devastating and beautiful. Justifies 8.4: exceptional character integration, great structure, very quotable.

jokes.text: And that would be different from the other times because...
jokes.setup: Moira objects that the garden tribute is tainted because her husband paid for it, unlike her other 'authentic' tributes.
jokes.punchline: The townsperson's trailing question — 'and that would be different from the other times because...' — implies that all of Moira's tributes were bought by someone, including her.
jokes.explanation: An exceptionally well-constructed puncturing of Moira's pretension. The trailing '...' does all the work — it's an incomplete question that the audience can complete in their heads, and the implication is perfectly devastating. Maximum economy. Justifies 8.0: the line accomplishes enormous comedic and character work in very few words.

jokes.text: I know this isn't what you wanted, but I think it would make Johnny really happy to make you happy. I mean, take Roland. Every year he buys me coconut macaroons, and I just don't have the heart to tell him I am really allergic to coconut. Yeah, every Valentine's Day I just rash right up.
jokes.setup: The townsperson is trying to convince Moira to accept the garden tribute for Johnny's sake.
jokes.punchline: The anecdote about Roland's annual coconut macaroons — and her severe allergy — is both a digression and a dark comedy about the cost of politeness.
jokes.explanation: The escalating allergy detail — rash, then 'throat almost completely shut' — is dark and funny. The anecdote works as both a character moment for the townsperson and a comedic aside that earns its place.

jokes.text: Last year my throat almost completely shut.
jokes.setup: She's been describing allergic reactions to Roland's annual macaroons.
jokes.punchline: The escalation to near-anaphylaxis — delivered casually, as if 'my throat almost shut' is just a detail — is the dark kicker.
jokes.explanation: The casual delivery of near-death is the joke. The contrast between the politeness context (not wanting to hurt Roland's feelings) and the severity (almost dying) is darkly comic and well-executed.

jokes.text: I shouldn'tve eaten those eggs. [Stevie: I can't believe I'm trapped under a blanket with you knowing you ate those eggs.]
jokes.setup: David and Stevie are under a blanket together; David has eaten the suspicious eggs from Stevie's fridge.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's horror at being trapped with someone whose digestion has been compromised by questionable eggs is a direct callback to the fridge scene.
jokes.explanation: Solid callback — the eggs pay off. Stevie's specificity ('trapped under a blanket... knowing you ate those eggs') is characteristically precise about her discomfort. Works well as a continued scene beat.

jokes.text: I bet Patrick's fridge is fully stocked. [Stevie: What does that mean?!] It means he's got his life together. He's a pretty eligible bachelor. Patrick.
jokes.setup: David and Stevie are lying under a blanket; David unpromptedly brings up Patrick.
jokes.punchline: David's non-sequitur praise of Patrick's fridge — 'he's got his life together,' 'pretty eligible bachelor' — while blanketed with Stevie after eating her bad eggs, is transparently not about fridge inventory.
jokes.explanation: The 'Patrick's fridge is fully stocked' is a funny non-sequitur that also plants seeds of David's growing feelings. 'He's a pretty eligible bachelor. Patrick.' — naming him at the end as if clarifying — is a lovely character beat showing David thinking about Patrick but trying to pass it off as casual observation.

jokes.text: What? He's my business partner. / Oh, aren't they all.
jokes.setup: David defensively insists Patrick is just his business partner.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's 'oh, aren't they all' implies David has a history of romanticizing business relationships — delivered with perfect knowing sarcasm.
jokes.explanation: 'Oh, aren't they all' is perfect Stevie deadpan — four words that suggest an entire history of David blurring professional and romantic lines. High economy, strong character work, and for viewers watching the David/Patrick arc develop, enormous rewatch value.

jokes.text: Oh, aren't they all.
jokes.setup: David insists his relationship with Patrick is purely professional.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's 'oh, aren't they all' is a perfect dry callback to David's history of blurring professional and personal lines.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's four-word response carries enormous weight: it references David's entire romantic history (including their own situation), calls out his pattern, and is devastatingly economical. Maximum character integration for Stevie — only she would deliver this with this timing.

jokes.text: Yeah, because we're in business together, and compliment each other professionally.
jokes.setup: Stevie suggests David and Patrick seem to get along very well.
jokes.punchline: 'Compliment each other professionally' — David meaning 'complement' (fit together) but possibly saying 'compliment' (praise each other) — is either a malapropism or an accidental truth.
jokes.explanation: The 'compliment/complement' ambiguity — if intentional — is a nice piece of writing that captures David's feelings slipping through his professional language. Whether accidental or deliberate, it's a small but rich moment.

jokes.text: Yes, because unlike you and I, he's extremely generous, and has absolutely no ulterior motive. [Stevie: Then why are you here and not there?] Because you asked me first. [Stevie: I didn't, though.]
jokes.setup: Stevie is pressing David on why he chose to come to her place rather than Patrick's.
jokes.punchline: David's claim that 'you asked me first' is immediately deflated by Stevie's flat 'I didn't, though' — confirming David chose to come here of his own accord.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's 'I didn't, though' is a perfect flat contradiction. The implication is that David chose her over Patrick for reasons he won't admit, and Stevie is gently making him confront that. Well-structured exchange.

jokes.text: Because we're in business together, and I don't know what his preferences are. [Stevie: Well, you're not gonna find out what his preferences are on a sleepover with me.]
jokes.setup: David is trying to explain why he can't stay at Patrick's.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's response — 'you're not gonna find out what his preferences are on a sleepover with me' — is double-edged: she's both pointing out the absurdity of his excuse and gently pushing him toward Patrick.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's line works on two levels: the surface joke (you won't learn his sleeping preferences from staying here) and the deeper implication (you won't find out if he's interested in you by hiding at my place). The double meaning is well-crafted. Stevie as knowing observer of David's feelings is perfectly deployed.

jokes.text: Guest bedroom, what is he, Bill Gates? [David: Yes, he's Bill Gates.]
jokes.setup: David mentions he wouldn't share a bed with Patrick, he'd be in the guest bedroom.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's skeptical 'what is he, Bill Gates?' (implying having a guest bedroom is extravagant), and David's flat 'yes, he's Bill Gates' — agreeing with the absurd comparison — is a quick, funny exchange.
jokes.explanation: David's deadpan 'yes, he's Bill Gates' is a perfectly dry response to an overstated comparison. The joke is in the commitment — David refuses to engage with the sarcasm and treats it as a genuine statement.

jokes.text: I like this for you. [David: Like what? There's nothing to like.] You seem flustered. [David: I'm not flus... Maybe it's the eggs.]
jokes.setup: Stevie has been pressing David about Patrick; David is increasingly defensive.
jokes.punchline: David's 'I'm not flus—' cut off mid-word, followed by 'maybe it's the eggs,' is a perfect comedic retreat — he physically cannot even complete 'flustered' before abandoning the denial and blaming his condition on the eggs.
jokes.explanation: The interrupted 'I'm not flus—' is a beautiful piece of comedy writing — the word literally can't be completed because David knows it's true. Then 'maybe it's the eggs' as a pivot is both a callback and a desperate face-saving move. This moment encapsulates David's emotional avoidance perfectly. High character integration: only David would retreat to blaming questionable eggs for his romantic flustering. Justifies 8.2.

jokes.text: Um, am I reading this right? 'The Moira's Rose's Garden.'
jokes.setup: Johnny presents the plaque for Moira's garden.
jokes.punchline: The plaque reads 'The Moira's Rose's Garden' — a grammatically tortured title that somehow adds an extra possessive and an article to create maximum confusion.
jokes.explanation: 'The Moira's Rose's Garden' is a great visual/verbal gag — the tortured grammar implies three possible interpretations (garden of Moira? Garden of Moira's rose? Garden belonging to a Rose named Moira?). Johnny's explanation ('confused the engraver') is the perfect flustered excuse. The plaque joke is well set up and lands very well. Justifies 8.1.

jokes.text: So the garden is dedicated to a rose that Moira owns?
jokes.setup: The family is trying to parse the grammar of 'The Moira's Rose's Garden.'
jokes.punchline: David's earnest first interpretation — a garden dedicated to a specific rose owned by Moira — is a logical reading of the catastrophic grammar.
jokes.explanation: David's logical unpacking of the impossible grammar is funny because it's actually a reasonable reading of the text. The character of David as someone who would immediately try to parse the semantics is perfectly deployed.

jokes.text: I don't think that there are enough apostrophes. [Beat] I don't even see a rose in the garden. [Beat] Because there aren't any roses. [Beat] Or are we the Roses?
jokes.setup: The family is unpacking the grammatical disaster of the plaque.
jokes.punchline: The escalating confusions — insufficient apostrophes, no actual roses, 'or are we the Roses?' — build on each other in quick succession.
jokes.explanation: The escalating family deconstruction of the plaque is a great group comedy sequence. 'I don't think there are enough apostrophes' is a funny complaint; the reveal of no roses makes it worse; 'or are we the Roses?' is the funny meta turn that acknowledges their family name.

jokes.text: Okay, I'm getting roses for the garden. I'm-I'm getting roses.
jokes.setup: David has noted there are no roses in the rose garden.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's repeated commitment — 'I'm getting roses, I'm-I'm getting roses' — delivered as a flustered stutter, is a funny image of a man patching a sinking ship.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's flustered 'I'm-I'm getting roses' is a functional character beat — the stutter conveys how overwhelmed he is by the compounding errors. Not exceptional but it works.

jokes.text: Those are the last four digits of your father's credit card.
jokes.setup: David asks what the numbers on the plaque are; Johnny says it's Moira's birthday.
jokes.punchline: Alexis reveals they're actually the last four digits of Johnny's credit card number — meaning Johnny filled in the plaque order form using whatever numbers were to hand.
jokes.explanation: The reveal that the plaque numbers are a credit card's last four digits — because Johnny confused the order form fields — is a genuinely great joke. It perfectly captures Johnny's endearing incompetence and the image of him fumbling through an online form in a panic. Alexis's flat delivery makes it land better. Justifies 8.2: unexpected, specific, and reveals character beautifully.

jokes.text: Okay, enough, enough. This is still a very big honor for your mother. Is it not? / Mhm... / Yes? / Mhm.
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to salvage the moment after the family has torn the plaque apart.
jokes.punchline: The weak 'mhm' responses from Moira and David — deeply unconvincing, reluctant agreement — undermine Johnny's optimism while he's still speaking.
jokes.explanation: A good quiet reaction beat — the reluctant affirmations that barely meet the bar of agreement. Solid but not especially inventive.

jokes.text: Um, I hope you got a really good deal on this. [Alexis: It's by the letter, so he paid extra for those apostrophe 'S's.']
jokes.setup: The plaque is already a disaster of grammar and credit card numbers.
jokes.punchline: The revelation that Johnny paid extra per letter — meaning the grammatically unnecessary apostrophe-S's cost him extra money — is the final escalation of the plaque fiasco.
jokes.explanation: The extra charge for unnecessary apostrophes is a perfect button to the plaque sequence. The information that Johnny paid more money for the grammatical errors adds financial insult to lexical injury. Alexis's delivery ('it's by the letter, so...') is perfectly matter-of-fact. Good structural payoff.

jokes.text: So when we die, are we all gonna be buried here?
jokes.setup: Johnny insists the garden is still a big honor for Moira despite all the problems.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's non-sequitur question — 'so when we die, are we all gonna be buried here?' — treats the garden as a family monument/cemetery in the most darkly literal way possible.
jokes.explanation: The episode's final joke is a great button. Alexis's leap from 'garden tribute' to 'family burial plot' is both absurd and the logical endpoint of the legacy conversation. The dark premise delivered by Alexis with total sincerity is very funny and ends the episode on a characteristic note. 'So when we die, are we all gonna be buried here?' is extremely quotable. Justifies 8.2: strong economy, great character integration, perfect placement as the final joke.

tmdb_overview: Johnny tries to avoid the spread of lice in the motel. Moira tries to get out of having a flower garden named after her.
insight: Character escalation carries a middling episode through mild cringe and repeated "lice" gags.
